chunk_summary: This section discusses standard, adjustable, and targeted fortification methods for expressed and donor milk, focusing on protein delivery, nutrient precision, and practical endpoints for preterm infant nutrition in NICUs.
learning_objectives: Understand the differences between standard, adjustable, and targeted fortification methods for human milk.
Learn how to interpret biochemical markers like BUN and creatinine to adjust fortification strategies.
Identify practical endpoints for de-escalation of fortification in preterm infants.
Explore post-discharge nutritional strategies for preterm infants to support growth and breastfeeding.
key_facts: Standard fortification uses a fixed amount of fortifier, such as a sachet for 20–25 mL of milk, but may under-deliver protein due to milk composition variability.
Adjustable fortification relies on biochemical markers like BUN (<8–10 mg/dL indicates inadequate protein; >16–20 mg/dL suggests excess protein or dehydration).
Targeted fortification uses milk analyzers to measure fat and protein content, allowing precise nutrient adjustments but requiring specialized equipment and staff.
De-escalation endpoints include term-equivalent age, consistent catch-up growth, and stable weight trajectory (around 2.0–2.5 kg).
Post-discharge options for faltering growth include fortified expressed breast milk (EBM) or nutrient-enriched post-discharge formula under supervision.
reference_quote: Fortification of human milk for preterm infants: update and recommendations of the European Milk Bank Association Working Group on human milk fortification.
document_key_topics: Standard fortification methods for human milk
Post-discharge nutritional strategies for preterm infants
